topic: Evidence hierarchy roadmap div (EXP-091) — How This Review Is Organized
our_argument_summary: The roadmap div inserted between the scorecard and Section 1.1 explains our three-tier evidence structure: Tier 1 (self-contradictions using dome's own parameters), Tier 2 (point-by-point WIN evaluation), Tier 3 (kill-shots and falsification tests using external data). It claims this ordering is deliberate because argument type determines how hard a conclusion is to escape. It references the dome's own 'Steelman Evidence Ladder' as context for our complementary structure.

holes_found.description: The roadmap states: 'The dome model presents its own evidence hierarchy — a Steelman Evidence Ladder ranking its strongest claims.' This is factually wrong. The dome model does NOT use the term 'Steelman Evidence Ladder' — this is OUR analytical construct from Part 6 (Predictions Page — Structural Analysis), where WE identified what we assess to be the dome's four strongest claims (STEELMAN-01 through STEELMAN-04: Australia Scaffold, Eclipse Magnetic Ensemble, Tesla Disc Lock, Single-Constant Unification). The dome's raw text contains no mention of 'Steelman', 'evidence ladder', 'evidence hierarchy', 'scaffold', or any formal ranking system. The dome site says only: 'The strongest claim this registry makes is prospective prediction' (02-wins.txt line 10). Attributing our own analytical label to the dome model is exactly the kind of strawmanning we criticize the dome for — putting words in someone's mouth. A dome defender could legitimately say: 'We never created a Steelman Evidence Ladder. You invented that term, attributed it to us, then used it as a framing device. This is misrepresentation.'
holes_found.recommendation: Rewrite the opening sentence. Options: (a) 'This review applies a three-tier structure organized by argument type rather than by topic' — simply remove the dome attribution. (b) 'The dome model implicitly prioritizes prospective predictions as its strongest evidence. This review applies a complementary structure...' — accurately describes the dome's actual stated priority without fabricating a label. (c) If we want to reference our own Steelman analysis: 'In Part 6, we identify the dome's four strongest claims and subject them to steelman analysis. Here, the broader review is organized by argument type...' — this correctly attributes the Steelman label to us.

holes_found.description: Tier 3 description potentially undersells kill-shots by framing them primarily through their vulnerability: 'These are powerful but rely on external datasets...that a determined defender could dispute as measured from the wrong frame.' While this is accurate about the rhetorical landscape, it reads as though we're apologizing for our own evidence. The roadmap's PURPOSE is to explain why self-contradictions are hardest to escape, not to signal-boost the dome's counterarguments. A dome defender reading this would be delighted: 'Even the reviewers admit their kill-shots depend on frame assumptions we dispute.' We should explain the hierarchy without handing the defender ammunition.
holes_found.recommendation: Reframe Tier 3 to lead with strength, then note the distinction: 'Structured tests where the dome and the globe make different, measurable predictions. These are independently devastating — Gaia parallax from 1.8 billion stars, GPS atomic clock corrections, Southern Hemisphere distances — but they rely on external datasets. The self-contradiction arguments in Tier 1 go further: they need no external data at all, using only the dome's own equations.' This preserves the hierarchy logic without sounding like we're undermining our own evidence.

holes_found.description: The Tier 1 examples are well-chosen but the Schumann frequency range is stated as '22–35 Hz' which, while supported by the Part 2 analysis (22.6 Hz linear average, 35 Hz area-weighted average), doesn't match the section title '2.1 Schumann Resonance: 7.83 Hz vs. ~22 Hz'. A reader clicking through from the roadmap to Section 2.1 sees '~22 Hz' in the heading, not '22–35 Hz'. This minor inconsistency could confuse careful readers and invite a defender to say 'they can't even keep their own numbers straight.'
holes_found.recommendation: Either use '~22 Hz' (matching the section title, using the most favorable linear average) or use 'at least 8.77 Hz at the pole and rising to 35 Hz' (matching the full analysis). The range '22–35 Hz' is technically the average range, which is defensible but should match what readers see when they click through.

holes_found.description: The roadmap is not wrapped in a <details>/<summary> progressive disclosure element, per EXP-091's own progressive_disclosure_note. This is intentional (EXP-091 notes it at ~280 words, short enough to be permanently visible). However, it's the ONLY substantial content block in Part 1 outside a <details> wrapper, which could look inconsistent. The EXP-091 author anticipated this and suggested the decider could optionally wrap it with open attribute. Not a real problem, just noting the design choice.
holes_found.recommendation: No action needed — the permanent visibility is the right UX choice for a roadmap. Readers need orientation before expanding anything.

holes_found.description: The closing paragraph states: 'A reader who trusts only the dome's own numbers and reads only Parts 2 and 2b will find the model already refutes itself.' This is a strong claim. Parts 2 and 2b contain self-contradictions and code analysis. The code analysis (Part 2b) discusses monitoring infrastructure tags (hardcoded, live_fetch, etc.) — these are NOT purely dome-parameter self-contradictions. Part 2b uses our own tags derived from analyzing the dome's code. So 'trusts only the dome's own numbers' is slightly overstated for Part 2b, which is our analysis of the dome's methodology rather than a pure self-contradiction from dome parameters.
holes_found.recommendation: Change to 'A reader who reads only Parts 2 and 2b will find the model already refutes itself — Part 2 from the dome's own geometry, Part 2b from the dome's own automation code.' This is more accurate about what each part contributes.

stronger_arguments.description: The roadmap could briefly mention that Parts 2 and 2b are sufficient to reach the review's conclusions, making everything after them confirmatory. This would make the self-sufficiency argument explicit and give time-pressed readers permission to stop at Part 2b with full confidence.
sources: HOLISTIC-HOL-HIERARCHY review recommendation: 'even if dome defenders dispute external data, the self-contradictions and unfalsifiability arguments stand'

stronger_arguments.description: Consider noting that the dome's own stated methodology prioritizes prospective prediction ('The strongest claim this registry makes is prospective prediction' — raw-text/02-wins.txt line 10). Our tier system is organized by argument TYPE (self-contradiction > interpretation challenge > external falsification), which is a different axis. Making this distinction explicit shows intellectual honesty and prevents conflation of the dome's evidence hierarchy (by topic/strength) with ours (by argument type).

kernel_of_truth.description: The roadmap addresses a genuine structural weakness identified by the holistic review (HOL-HIERARCHY): readers had no explicit guidance on why our review is organized the way it is, or why self-contradictions are categorically stronger than external falsifications. The three-tier taxonomy is sound and well-reasoned. The specific examples (Schumann, tides) are excellent choices — concrete, accessible, and genuinely devastating.
kernel_of_truth.why_holes_matter: The critical hole (misattributing the Steelman Evidence Ladder to the dome) is exactly the kind of error we attack the dome for — claiming an opponent said something they didn't. If we strawman the dome's organizational claims, we undermine our credibility as fair-minded reviewers. The fix is simple (remove or correctly attribute the reference) and doesn't weaken the roadmap's actual argument at all.

citations_verified: Schumann frequency 7.83 Hz — correct observed fundamental (verified against Part 2.1)
Moon altitude 2,534 km — matches dome core parameters in CLAUDE.md
22–35 Hz range — supported by Part 2.1 analysis (22.6 Hz linear average, 35 Hz area-weighted)
Two-bulge tidal pattern — correct description of observed semidiurnal tides (verified against Part 2.2)
citations_failed: Steelman Evidence Ladder — attributed to dome model but this term does not exist in any dome raw text (01-home.txt through 08-live-power.txt). It is OUR analytical construct from Part 6.

deeper_analysis:
The evidence-roadmap div is a well-motivated structural improvement responding to a real gap identified by the HOL-HIERARCHY holistic review. The three-tier framework (self-contradictions > point-by-point evaluation > external falsification) is logically sound and reflects the genuine epistemological hierarchy: arguments from internal consistency are harder to escape than arguments from external data.

The critical problem is the Steelman Evidence Ladder misattribution. This is not a minor phrasing issue — it's a factual claim about what the dome model contains. I searched all eight dome raw-text files (01-home.txt through 08-live-power.txt) for 'steelman', 'evidence ladder', 'evidence hierarchy', 'scaffold', 'ranking', and found nothing. The dome site's only statement about evidence priority is: 'The strongest claim this registry makes is prospective prediction' (02-wins.txt:10). The STEELMAN-01 through STEELMAN-04 analyses in our Part 6 are OUR construction — we identified what we consider the dome's four strongest claims and subjected them to steelman analysis. Attributing this back to the dome is circular: we created the Steelman framework, then the roadmap claims the dome 'presents' it.

To be fair to the analyst who wrote EXP-091: the expansion's own text is more careful. The 'kernel_of_truth' section says 'The dome model has its own evidence hierarchy' (vague but arguably true — the dome does prioritize certain claims) and the replacement_html says 'The dome model presents its own evidence hierarchy — a Steelman Evidence Ladder ranking its strongest claims.' The transition from 'has its own evidence hierarchy' (defensible) to 'presents... a Steelman Evidence Ladder' (fabricated attribution) happened in the HTML text itself. The EXP-091 analyst may have intended to describe the concept and accidentally promoted our label to a dome feature.

The moderate issue about Tier 3 framing is worth addressing because it reveals a tension in the roadmap's rhetorical strategy. The roadmap wants to explain WHY self-contradictions are stronger than kill-shots, which requires acknowledging the kill-shots' theoretical vulnerability. But the way it's phrased ('a determined defender could dispute as measured from the wrong frame') reads as an invitation to the dome defender. Better to phrase the hierarchy positively (Tier 1 needs no external data) than negatively (Tier 3 can be disputed).

On the other side: a dome defender reading this roadmap would actually find it frustrating. The roadmap tells them upfront that the review's strongest arguments use their own parameters — before they even start reading. This is strategically sound. The misattribution is the one gift we give them; removing it makes the roadmap airtight.
